paragraphs.text: About eighty miles due northwes[t] of
that has all the combine[d] romance of the ages in its hist[o]ry[.] I[t] is rarely visited and has but
, yet it has peculiar fa[s]cination for many people. The very elements seem to conspire to make the place forbidden, as it almost invariably blows a gale of wind in the vicinity and when one reaches
there is no bay or harbor for [a] craft much larger than a rowboat.
is about seven miles long by two or three in width and rises to a height of 800 to 1,000 feet in the center.

Wind swept, forbidding, haunted by the spirits of storms, it does not invite inspection and appear[s] to hav[e] been cur[s]ed by the elements, yet despite this the island was at one tim[e] the home of a numerous people[,]
a race that has been lost sight of

and wa[s] literally blown off, finally making [it] one day about noon, coming to anchor near a long, specter-like tongue of land that extends out into the ocean[,] where the tide ran like a mill race and a heavy ground swell swept around the point form the windward side. The
appears like a sand dune. The landing was a wave swept beach that received a high swell from three points, and it was evident that skill in landing alone would make it possible.
lay off, waiting for a low sea, and finally went sailing in upon one of less size, and
jumped overboard and held on to
that the undertow and back rush attempted to claim. In this manner

He had not heard the news of the world for a year and did not display any dispositon to learn it, but he was not happy—too many dead people, too many bones that make wind spirits blow, he said, and when he heard that

was after the remains of these dead people he showed an inclination to leave. In all probability there is not in any land a more remarkable mound to be seen. About ten feet in height and nearly a [mile] in length, it was at this time a veritable Golgotha, a collection of bones and implements of
[,] ever changing, ever shifting, toda[y] exposing numbers of skeletons, tomorrow covering them up in sheets of sand.

The mound was a town site upon which hundreds of people had lived for untold ages. They had brought in their shells and fish bones[,] piling them up at the doors of their huts, slowly building themselves up higher and higher, until in time they came to live on the the top of a vast shell mound. Here they buried their dead, concealed their household goods, and when a man or woman died his or her possessions were buried with them, and so the big mounds were made up of shells, stone implements, fish and human bones—a monument of
came from, who they were or why they lived on such
a wind swept, nearly waterless island
is one of the mysteries, but it is known that the
and divided them up among the various mainland tribes. When
was being carried into execution, a storm came up, and in the hurry of departure it was found that
It was impossible to land, so
She had plunged into the waves and swum a long distance to the shore and with
intending to return later for her.
, however, was not visited for many years.
was wrecked, but the legend lived, and many years after
had survived. An expedition was got up in 1850,
making a safe landing. They formed a line across
in order not to miss any signs of human habitation and began a slow search from the south point north. They soon found evidence of ancient occupation and in a deep canyon discovered a hut and
, the mother who had been deserted years before. She was living in the hut, which was made of whale ribs and sea lion hair, her sole companions being several wild dog[s] that savagely charged
could not make herself understood, and, singularly enough, no one in all the Indian tribes in southern
could be found who understood her.
by signs that the wild dogs had killed
long ago and that she killed sea lions and sea birds with stones and lived on them.
At first she appeared to be afraid of

; but, finding that they treated her kindly, she consented to go with them. She took all the rude articles she had used during the years of her solitude, her skin dresses, made mostly from the skins of the sea lions, and so interesting were the costumes that they were sent to
which accomplished her rescue[.] Sh[e] was placed in care of
, who did everything possible for h[e]r.
She was bright and vivacious in disposition, learned some Spani[s]h, danced and s[a]ng f[or] her friends and was visited by Indians from various parts of
in an effort to find some one who could understand her.

But civilization proved too much for her[,] and she died in three months[,] one of the most remarkable Crusoes known[,] as her story is based on actual facts and in its details is much stronger [stranger] than fiction.
was formed during past centuries by
and possesses an intense interest on this account.

near the landing was twenty or thirty feet higher than the shore and reached through singular wind worn passes, wind being the sculptor here[.] Once on the heights a broad mesa was seen[,] as level as a floor and marked with lines which resembled ancient plowing or something of the kind[.] The mesa was covered with fine flinty stones about th[e] size of peas[,] which
says were picked up by the wind and blown through the air at times. They were arranged like chaff on a [beach.]
followed this mesa for several miles, and that
had had a large [and] vigorous population at [some] distant time was evident.

Every short distance some object was found, now a pipe made from the vertebr[a]e of a fish, a needle of fish bone, a dish made from a shell, the holes plugged with asphaltum[.] Here would be a stone club or mortar of steatite, a spearhead or a sinker and shell hooks that some fisherman c[en]turies ago perhaps had dropped and forgot.
Everywhere piles of big pearly shells of a size unknown here were found, telling of the lives and work of

. One mound was found covered by the ribs of whales and beneath them a f[l]at rock bearing marks of various kinds, and then the owner, a skeleton in perfect preservation, and all about it in the sand vessels of stone, flutes of bird bones and the various objects which went to make up the home of this unknown[.] In one spot six layers of skeletons were found, the lowest apparently very old[.] The question of age is always very interesting[,] but no one in contemplating these great shell deposits can form an adequate idea of their age[.]
evidently has been inhabited since the earliest times[.] But the elements have conspired against it, and it is doomed to be swept and covered with sand[.]
is now frequented by the professional collectors, who go over from

and live here for weeks and months, camping out on the sand dunes, weighting their tents down with rocks to prevent being blown into the [sea.] Every morning they go out, with their [h]ooks and poles, hunting for the spoils which have been uncovered by the wind in the night[.] In this way tons of material have been collected here and sent all over the world.
